intro.headline: Map your Renaissance baseline
intro.body: This short assessment estimates how your strengths are distributed across eight cross-domain capabilities. You will get an archetype, a skill profile, and recommended next steps.
bullets: Best used as a first-pass baseline

domains.label: Leadership
domains.description: Ability to align people, create clarity, and move decisions forward.

domains.label: Creativity
domains.description: Ability to generate novel approaches, ideas, and reframings.

domains.label: Strategy
domains.description: Ability to sequence action, model tradeoffs, and see leverage.

domains.label: Tech Proficiency
domains.description: Practical fluency with tools, systems, and technical workflows.

domains.label: Problem Solving
domains.description: Ability to decompose complexity and get to workable solutions.

domains.label: Critical Thinking
domains.description: Ability to question assumptions, weigh evidence, and reason rigorously.

domains.label: Adaptability
domains.description: Ability to adjust plans and behavior under changing conditions.

domains.label: Data Analysis
domains.description: Ability to use evidence, measurement, and quantitative signal.

archetypes.label: The Polymath
archetypes.description: A broad, balanced synthesizer with unusual pattern transfer across domains.

archetypes.label: The Strategist
archetypes.description: A systems-oriented thinker who links evidence, tradeoffs, and long-range direction.

archetypes.label: The Builder
archetypes.description: An execution-heavy profile that turns ideas into tools, systems, and outputs.

archetypes.label: The Leader
archetypes.description: A people-and-direction profile that creates clarity and adapts well under uncertainty.

questions.prompt: You are starting a new project with vague goals and incomplete context. What do you do first?

options.label: Map the outcome, constraints, and likely tradeoffs before acting.
Open the relevant tools and build a rough prototype to learn by doing.
Talk to the key people involved and align on expectations and ownership.
Generate several unconventional ways the problem could be framed before choosing a path.

questions.prompt: A team is stuck between two competing approaches. Which move sounds most like you?

options.label: Facilitate a decision, get alignment, and assign a clear owner.
Compare the assumptions, evidence, and downside risks in each option.
Run a quick test, simulation, or demo so reality can decide faster.
Reframe the problem so the team can see a third option they have not considered.

questions.prompt: You notice a workflow is repetitive, frustrating, and error-prone. What is your instinct?

options.label: Automate it, template it, or simplify it through tooling.
Measure where time and errors are actually occurring before changing anything.
Clarify roles, expectations, and handoffs so the team operates more smoothly.
Redesign the process from first principles rather than patching it.

questions.prompt: You need to learn a new domain fast. Which approach feels most natural?

options.label: Jump into a real tool or project and learn through direct experimentation.
Study the underlying concepts and models before trying to apply them.
Talk to experienced people and synthesize what actually works from their patterns.
Explore adjacent fields first so you can import surprising analogies and ideas.

questions.prompt: You need to persuade skeptical stakeholders to support a recommendation. What do you lean on most?

options.label: A clear narrative about priorities, tradeoffs, and long-term direction.
The evidence, benchmark data, and the logic behind the recommendation.
A concrete demo, workflow, or working proof that shows it can be done.
An original framing or analogy that makes the opportunity suddenly obvious.

questions.prompt: The situation changes mid-project and your original plan no longer fits. What do you do?

options.label: Adjust scope fast and keep momentum with the best workable version.
Pause long enough to re-check assumptions and verify what signal is still reliable.
Realign the people involved around the next most important decision.
Explore alternate directions others may be missing and choose the strongest pivot.

questions.prompt: How do you usually know a solution actually worked?

options.label: People are aligned, confusion is lower, and execution becomes smoother.
The metrics improved against a baseline or expected outcome.
The system became faster, cleaner, or more reliable in practice.
It opened up a stronger long-term path or strategic option than before.

questions.prompt: You hear a bold claim in a meeting or article. What is your first instinct?

options.label: Probe the assumptions, evidence quality, and hidden incentives behind it.
Map the second-order effects and how it changes the broader system.
See how you would test it in practice or build a small version of it.
Connect it to patterns from completely different fields and see what transfers.

questions.prompt: A capable colleague is struggling with a new assignment. How do you tend to help?

options.label: Clarify priorities, remove blockers, and help them regain momentum.
Give them a framework for making tradeoffs and choosing among options.
Create a checklist, template, or tool that makes the work easier to execute.
Brainstorm alternate approaches with them until a more original path appears.

questions.prompt: At the end of a project, what kind of reflection feels most useful?

options.label: Review the evidence, signal quality, mistakes, and what the team missed.
Capture the repeatable system or playbook so the next round starts stronger.
Reflect on how coordination, ownership, and decision-making worked across people.
Identify the most surprising ideas worth exploring further next time.
